coredata.dc:description: Electrolytic water splitting for hydrogen has rapidly developed along with the increasing necessity for clean energy resources. Meanwhile, the obstacle originating from the slow 4-electron-involved oxygen evolution reaction (OER) pushes the urgency to create efficient, durable, earth-abundant electrocatalysts. As one of the promising candidates, it remains a challenge to enhance the reactivity of the Prussian-blue analogue (PBA) towards the OER. Herein, we propose a unique tandem surface reconstruction strategy for NiFe PBA followed by heteroatomic F doping and graphene quantum dot (GQD) decoration to modify the surface chemistry. GQD could improve electrocatalytic performance through the higher transition metal interaction with NiFe PBA due to the distinct electronic structures and an abundance of edge sites. X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) revealed that F- ions were more likely to affect metal bonds. In contrast, GQD plays a role in modifying the metal states through the higher transition metal interaction. The proposed GQD/F-NiFe PBA with tandem surface engineering demonstrated promising electrocatalytic activity for OER in an alkaline medium, with an overpotential of 318 mV at a current density of 50 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 34.7 mV dec-1. The proposed tandem surface engineering for modifying PBA-based material surfaces represents a novel approach to designing advanced electrocatalysts.

(a) The OER polarization curves, (b) the double-layer capacitance (Cdl), (c) the Tafel slope, and (d) the chemical composition (Ni, Fe, F) in the atomic ratio (at%) of pristine NiFe PBA and F-NiFe PBA with various F doping concentrations.

(a) LSV curves, (b) the overpotential comparison of current at current density at 50 and 100 mA cm−2, (c) the atomic ratio of F, and (d) the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of F-NiFe PBA with various GQD concentrations.

XAS characterizations: (a) Ni K-edge XAS of NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, GQD/F-NiFe with Ni foil and NiO as the references; (b) Fe K-edge XAS of NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, GQD/F-NiFe with Fe foil and Fe3O4 as the references; k2-weighted FT-EXAFS R-space for (c) Ni and (d) Fe K-edge of NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, GQD/F-NiFe PBA.
(a) XPS Ni 2p spectra and (b) XPS Fe 2p spectra of F-NiFe PBA and GQD/F-NiFe PBA.

(a) The OER polarization curves and (b) the overpotential comparison of current density at 50 and 100 mA cm−2 of NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, GQD/NiFe PBA, GQD/F-NiFe PBA, and commercial RuO2; (c) The Nyquist plots, (d) the Tafel slope, and (e) the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, GQD/NiFe PBA, and GQD/F-NiFe PBA; (f) Chronopotentiometry measurement of GQD/F-NiFe PBA at a high current density of 100 mA cm−2 for 30 h under 1.0 M KOH.
Schematic illustration of the synthesized processes of GQD-modified F-NiFe PBA via tandem surface engineering.

head.ce:title: Tandem surface engineering of graphene quantum dot-assisted fluorinated NiFe Prussian blue analogue for electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrolytic water splitting for hydrogen has rapidly developed along with the increasing necessity for clean energy resources. Meanwhile, the obstacle originating from the slow 4-electron-involved oxygen evolution reaction (OER) pushes the urgency to create efficient, durable, earth-abundant electrocatalysts. As one of the promising candidates, it remains a challenge to enhance the reactivity of the Prussian-blue analogue (PBA) towards the OER. Herein, we propose a unique tandem surface reconstruction strategy for NiFe PBA followed by heteroatomic F doping and graphene quantum dot (GQD) decoration to modify the surface chemistry. GQD could improve electrocatalytic performance through the higher transition metal interaction with NiFe PBA due to the distinct electronic structures and an abundance of edge sites. X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) revealed that F- ions were more likely to affect metal bonds. In contrast, GQD plays a role in modifying the metal states through the higher transition metal interaction. The proposed GQD/F-NiFe PBA with tandem surface engineering demonstrated promising electrocatalytic activity for OER in an alkaline medium, with an overpotential of 318 mV at a current density of 50 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 34.7 mV dec-1. The proposed tandem surface engineering for modifying PBA-based material surfaces represents a novel approach to designing advanced electrocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The world population increased at an unprecedented rate, leading to a rise in energy consumption, prosperity, and a corresponding rise in industrialization [1]. Nearly all agree that reaching the net-zero objective by 2040 requires rapid progress in the research and development of renewable energy sources to substitute traditional fossil fuels. Electrochemical water splitting is one of the most promising technologies for a future in which renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar, comprise the vast majority of power production [2]. Due to their high catalytic activity in both acidic and alkaline environments, commercial electrocatalysts, such as RuO2 and IrO2 for the oxygen evolution process (OER), are considered current developments and benchmark electrocatalysts for electrochemical water splitting [3]. In contrast, their practicable applications are constrained by the great scarcity and the expensive cost of the noble metals needed [4]. The development of highly efficient and stable, low-cost electrocatalysts has been the subject of substantial and intense study in this field of research [5,6].

The class of metal–organic framework families known as Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) with open frameworks is distinguished by the presence of transition metals (TMs) that are ordered and linked with the ligands of the cyan group (CN–) [7,8]. The variety of TM ions makes it possible for PBAs to possess completely modifiable physicochemical features and exhibit their ability to have prospective uses in OER [9–13]. PBAs are now being extensively used as potent precursors in the production of a wide variety of nanomaterials, including sulfides [14], brides [15], nitrides [16], oxides [17], and phosphides [18]. However, PBAs often have limited activity toward water-splitting reactions, which can result in poor overall efficiency. The inefficiency is because PBAs typically have low surface area and poor electrical conductivity [19]. At the same time, pyrolysis at high temperatures drives an unforeseen demise of the framework structures of PBAs, which subsequently transform and contribute to a significant aggregation of their derivatives. It prevents the full utilization of PBA structural advantages [20].

Several studies have been conducted on PBA surface modifications, with some exploring strategies to boost bulk materials reactivity, such as the doping process, which includes sulfide, phosphide, and chemical vapor deposition [21]. In recent years, there has been an increasing interest in fluorine doping for catalytic applications in water splitting, particularly in OER. Ma et al. pursued high temperatures during pyrolysis to reconstruct the PBA surfaces, exposing more active PBA sites [22]. With a greater electronegativity than other nonmetallic atoms, the F atoms generated through fluorine-doped have a greater tendency to form weak ionic bonds of metal-fluorine, which proves beneficial for surface reconstruction and dramatically enhances the surface energy of the crystal plane [23]. Moreover, it has been observed that using fluorine-doped by itself may not improve the catalyst’s electrical conductivity. In some instances, it may even lead to the formation of aggregates on the catalysts, thus changing the surface chemistry of the catalysts [24].

Furthermore, graphene quantum dots (GQD) had potential qualities for effective OER electrocatalysts due to their nano-sized diameters of graphene fragments (5–20 nm) [25]. Due to their nano-sized, GQD can also provide uniform coverage of the intended substance. GQD has been reported to be used as a composite or coating material in energy storage systems [26]. Lin et al. investigated the extensive lattice mismatch of GQD to accelerate the electron transport in NiFe PBA further [27]. Moreover, Rinawati et al. used the abundant active and edge sites of GQD to enhance the MOF-derived LDH catalytic activity [28]. More importantly, GQD exhibit remarkable catalytic properties due to their large surface area, unique electronic structure, and numerous edge sites [29]. By incorporating GQD into electrocatalytic systems, the electrical conductivity can be improved, leading to enhanced kinetic transport properties and providing excellent stability of catalysts [30–34].

Herein, we propose that GQD/F-NiFe was generated using tandem surface engineering strategies: the first strategy involved surface reconstruction through the facile hydrothermal process of fluorination on NiFe PBA surfaces. The second strategy involved F-NiFe PBA modification by introducing GQD, illustrated in Scheme 1 . The F- ions may participate in the electronic structure change without changing the framework structure of NiFe PBA, while the GQD will enhance the electrocatalytic activity. GQD/F-NiFe PBA has exhibited an overpotential of 318 mV at 50 mA cm−2 for OER and remains stable under a high current density. It is interesting to bring up that the fluorine doping that will reconstruct the surface of NiFe PBA and could support the GQD would alter the surface chemistry of F-NiFe PBA, which is favorable to increasing the electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic OER performance. The synergistic interactions of surface reconstruction from F- ions and higher surface oxidation of GQD bring about a tremendously stable structure, boosting the performance of NiFe PBA.

Nickel(II)nitrate hexahydrate ((Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) ≥ 98 %) was obtained from Alfa Aesar. Potassium hexacyanoferrate (K3(Fe(CN)6) ≥ 99.0 %) was purchased from J.T. Baker. Trisodium citrate dihydrate ((C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) ≥ 99 %) from ACROS was used, and Nafion 117 solution was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ruthenium oxide hydrate (with 99.99 % purity) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Sigma-Aldrich supplied potassium hydroxide (KOH). Ammonium fluoride (NH4F ≥ 98 %) and citric acid ((C6H8O7) ≥ 99.5 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

First, a 0.264 g amount of potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) was dissolved in 60 mL DI water to form solution A. Next, 0. 348 g of nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate and 0.441 g of trisodium citrate dihydrate were dissolved into 40 mL DI water to form solution B. Then, solution A was added to solution B under vigorous stirring for 24 h. After stirring for 24 h, the co-precipitation was obtained by centrifugation, washed with DI water, and dried in a drying oven overnight at 70 °C.

To synthesize F-doped NiFe PBA, several concentrations of NH4F (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 M) were mixed with a 0.40 g of co-precipitation of NiFe PBA independently in 40 mL DI water and sonicated for 15 min. Then, the resultant solution was put into a 100 mL autoclave with Teflon-lined for 2 h at 150 °C via hydrothermal treatment. Next, the product was obtained by centrifugation, washed with DI water, and dried in a drying oven for 6 h at 70 °C. The best result was obtained from F-NiFe PBA with 0.01 M of NH4F.

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) was prepared via hydrothermal treatment. A 0.40 g of citric acid was added to 40 mL DI water and sonicated for 30 min. Next, the solution was put into a 100 mL autoclave with Teflon-lined for 3 h at 200 °C. After the hydrothermal treatment, the clear solution was dialyzed with DI water for 20 min using a dialysis tube (Spectrum Lab. Inc, 3000 Da) to separate it from the impurities.

The method was the same as prepared F-NiFe PBA with several concentrations of NH4F (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 M) mixed with a 0.40 g of co-precipitation of NiFe PBA independently in 40 mL DI water and sonicated for 15 min. Then, the resultant solution was put into a 100 mL autoclave with Teflon-lined for 2 h at 150 °C via hydrothermal treatment. After the hydrothermal treatment, cool the solution at room temperature. Next, 1 mL of GQD solution was added into the F-NiFe PBA solution independently and mixed under vigorous stirring for 2 h at room temperature. The final product was centrifuged, washed with DI water, and dried in an oven for 6 h at 70 °C. The best result was obtained from GQD/F-NiFe PBA with 0.05 M of NH4F and 1 mL of GQD concentration.

The Ni foam was used as the electrode for assessing OER performance (FuCell Co., Ltd., Taoyuan, Taiwan). Ni foam (1 cm × 3 cm) was cleaned with 1 M HCl by ultrasonication for 30 min, then washed with DI water several times. After cleaning, the cleaned Ni foam was dried on a hotplate at 85 °C. The active electrode region for OER is 1 cm × 1 cm. Next, 10 mg of electrocatalyst was dispersed in 750 μL (isopropyl alcohol/ IPA), 250 μL (DI water), and 50 μL (Nafion 117 solution) to make the electrocatalyst slurry. To further homogenize the electrocatalyst slurry, ultrasonication was conducted for 15 min. The electrocatalyst slurry was dropped on the Ni foam with the loading amount of 3.0 mg cm−2 and dried on the hotplate at 80 °C.

The surface morphology characterization was conducted using an X-ray diffractometer (D2 Phaser, Bruker) with Cu K radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) to identify the crystallinity of materials. A field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6500F, JEOL) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, detector model 7418, Oxford). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, PerkinElmer, USA) was utilized to analyze the functional groups of the materials with the region limited to 500 – 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, SPECS PHOIBOS-150) was used to analyze the electronic states of the materials at the 24A1 beamline, and Ni and Fe K-edge of X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy was utilized to analyze the electronic and local structure of the materials at the 17C1 beamline, National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC) in Hsinchu, Taiwan.

All the electrochemical measurements were investigated using a three-electrode cell system, O2-saturated 1.0 M KOH at room temperature under controlled by Autolab potentiostat/ galvanostat (PGSTAT302 N, FRA32 M, Metrohm Autolab). The working electrode was Ni foam (1 cm × 1 cm) with a catalyst coated onto Ni foam. The platinum foil (1 cm × 4 cm) was used as the counter electrode, and Hg/HgO (1.0 M NaOH) as the reference electrode, respectively. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted at the scan rate of 5 mV s−1 with iR correction. Meanwhile, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in the frequency region of 100 kHz-0.1 Hz with 5 mV of amplitude. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was measured from the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) by performing the cyclic voltammetry (CV) at several scan rates (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mVs−1). The durability test was obtained from chronopotentiometry measurement with the overpotential at the current density of 100 mA cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization of NiFe PBA and F-NiFe PBA
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of the NiFe PBA was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as revealed in Fig. 1
a. The morphology of NiFe PBA shows a smooth nanocubes surface with an average particle size is about 150 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorine-doping on NiFe PBA (F-NiFe PBA) induced the transformation of the NiFe PBA cubical shape into irregular due to the hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 b) [22,35]. This phenomenon could happen due to the ion exchange on the F-NiFe PBA surface, leading to reconstruction [36]. Afterward, the crystallographic structure was confirmed by X-ray diffraction powder (XRD), and the prominent diffraction peaks after fluorination were slightly broadened on F-NiFe PBA. The broadened peaks appear at 17.0° and 25.0° after fluorine doping, which may indicate the strong electronegativity of the ionic exchange of F- ions and interaction with Ni3(Fe(CN)6)2 to the characteristic peaks of NiFe PBA (JCPDS card No. 86–0501) [37]. Although the reconstruction significantly changed the morphology of NiFe PBA, the XRD pattern was still consistent with NiFe PBA structure, even though it was a little broader. Moreover, it may be the main reason for the reconstruction in the F-NiFe PBA morphology [38,39]. On the other hand, the weak peak intensity may be due to the irregular shape of the F-NiFe PBA that decreases the crystallinity, as shown in Fig. 1 c [39,40]. The mechanism of fluorination doping may influence the powerful modulating effect of ammonium fluoride that can influence the chemical composition. In contrast, the NH4 + ion replaced the K+ site and originated from Fe (K3(Fe (CN)6)). The metal composition of Fe is significantly less than that of Ni, as shown in Fig. 1 d. The metal composition of pristine NiFe PBA (Ni and Fe) was 8.3 and 6.46 (at. %) before fluorine doping, which is much higher than 7.6 and 5.23 (at. %) after fluorine doping. The decrease in metal composition suggests that the reconstruction that happened on F-NiFe PBA may be caused by F- ions [41,42]. Additionally, the SEM images in Fig. S1 a-d showed that increasing fluorine concentration from 0.01 to 0.2 M will make the structure more irregular. The ion exchange during the hydrothermal process generates the reconstruction of NiFe PBA [43]. An energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) has provided evidence that the successful transformation of fluorine-doped NiFe PBA was carried out effectively (Fig. S1 e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic OER performance of F-NiFe PBA with various F-doping levels
ce:section.ce:para:
A further study was conducted to investigate the effect of surface reconstruction of NiFe PBA by the fluorination process on the OER performance. Fig. 2
a demonstrates the effect of fluorine doping concentration for F-NiFe PBA on the electrochemical OER performance using the LSV curve. The 0.01F-NiFe PBA catalyst exhibits an OER overpotential of 362 mV at the current density of 50 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, which is slightly lower than those of 0.03F-NiFe PBA (376 mV), 0.05F-NiFe PBA (379 mV), 0.1 F-NiFe PBA (400 mV), and the pristine NiFe PBA (423 mV). However, the electrocatalytic performance of 0.2 F-NiFe PBA (445 mV) was poor. It may imply that the higher concentration of fluorine may be responsible for the poor electrocatalytic performance of OER, and it could be suggested that the concentration of 0.01 M for the fluorine-doped NiFe PBA is the most effective. Afterward, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) derived from the cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements at several scan rates provides an approximate assessment of the electrochemical surface area (ECSA), which is indirectly proportional to the number of active sites [43–45]. The highest Cdl values are observed in 0.01F-NiFe PBA, which may have more exposed active sites due to its larger active surface area (Fig. 2 b). Next, as shown in Fig. 2 c, the corresponding Tafel slope of 0.01F-NiFe PBA is 60.26 mV dec-1, lower than that of NiFe PBA (144.23 mV dec-1). The lower value indicates that the OER kinetics was enhanced after fluorination [46]. To evaluate the possible reasons for OER performance, the atomic ratio calculation was performed in Fig. 2 d. Increasing the concentration of fluorine may influence the metal composition, which can be observed from the lower to the higher fluorine concentration. Moreover, the OER performance was decreased as the concentration of F in NiFe PBA increased. It is believed that the metal functions as the active sites and that an increase in F concentrations will result in a substantial loss of the metals as active sites. The increase might be due to the rising percentage of ion exchange reactions between NiFe PBA and the concentration of ammonium fluoride during hydrothermal processes, lowering the metal composition (Ni and Fe) and decreasing the OER performance. Thus, the above result suggests that 0.01 M was the optimum concentration of F that enhanced the OER performance.

The GQD/F-NiFe PBA was synthesized by physical mixing, and the structural characterization of graphene quantum dots (GQD)-modified F-doped NiFe PBA was investigated via the HRTEM image in Fig. 3
a. The EDX elemental mapping of the relevant GQD/F-NiFe PBA (Fig. 3 b) indicates that all elements Ni, Fe, F, and O are homogeneously distributed. The distribution of the oxygen could give us the rough illustration of the oxygen functional group that is mainly contributed from the GQD [47]. The lattice spacing of 0.539 nm and 0.292 nm (shown in Figure S2) could be well aligned to the (200) and (400) crystallography plane of the cubic NiFe PBA structure, and in agreement with those results of the XRD. Afterward, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were executed to define the functional groups of GQD/F-NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, and NiFe PBA electrocatalyst samples are shown in Figure S3. It was found that the peaks at (3600 and 3800 cm−1) and (2000 and 2200 cm−1) can be observed in the stretching vibrations of Fe-CN and Fe-CN-Ni [22,48,49]. Interestingly, a new peak belongs to stretching vibration of C-F bonds after introducing fluorine doping into F-NiFe PBA and GQD/F-NiFe PBA samples was confirmed at peak 1400 cm−1, respectively [50]. The weakening of the stretching vibration of Fe-CN, Fe-CN-Ni, and C-F bonds following the integration of GQD into F-doped NiFe PBA suggests the successful integration of GQD into the material mentioned above. In addition, Raman spectra were performed to observe the GQD existence in the final product of GQD/F-NiFe PBA. The spectral analysis from Fig. S4
a confirmed that the noticeable peaks corresponding to the C-F bond and (CN–) cyanide functional groups appear consistently in the F-NiFe PBA and GQD/F-NiFe PBA samples, showing the successful doping of the fluorine into NiFe PBA [51,52]. Meanwhile, two distinctive peaks belonging to the Dband and Gband appear in the GQD and GQD/F-NiFe PBA samples. In addition, the peak shift also happened in the CN– peak (Fig. S4 c), which belongs to the GQD/F-NiFe PBA, into a higher Raman shift PBA (2183 cm−1), indicating a structural change induced by the GQD in the F-NiFe PBA [27].

To explore the potential effect of GQD-modified F-NiFe PBA structure, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the GQD/F-NiFe PBA morphologies after being combined with several GQD concentrations of 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1 mL are presented in Figure S5. Interestingly, after being combined with 1 mL of GQD, the morphology of the sample changed significantly. As demonstrated in Fig. S5
a-d, the cubical morphology will become smaller while remaining in the NiFe PBA structure, and the irregular shapes will become more regular, decreasing particle size. Furthermore, the XRD patterns were investigated after adding the GQD to the F-NiFe PBA. The main characteristic peaks are fascinating to observe, and the XRD patterns reveal that after the GQD was added to the F-NiFe PBA, the main characteristic peaks remained in the NiFe PBA peaks (Figure S6). This indicates that the GQD did not change the structure of the F-NiFe PBA; rather, it will only prompt the peak intensity to decrease as the GQD concentrations are increased, especially for 1 mL of GQD concentration, which the particle size will follow decreases. On the other hand, as additional GQD is added to a solution of PBA throughout the synthesis process, the solution’s capacity to prevent the crystallization process increases along with its concentration. It may be a possible reason why the irregular cubic will gradually become regular cubic after increasing the concentration of GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic OER performance of GQD/F-NiFe PBA with various GQD concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performance was investigated for further studies to comprehend the surface of F-NiFe PBA modified by GQD. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, the electrocatalytic OER performance was observed via the LSV curves. This figure shows that the concentration of 1 mL GQD exhibits a better OER performance. Increasing the concentration of GQD results in a considerable increase in the current density, which implies that increasing the concentration of GQD would enhance faster electron transport. The corresponding overpotentials comparison at the current density of 50 mA cm−2 was obtained from the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves shown in Fig. 4 b, along with those at 100 mA cm−2. Clearly, GQD-1/0.01F-NiFe PBA also exhibited better OER performance with an overpotential 318 mV at 50 mA cm−2 and 348 mV at 100 mA cm−2, confirming the great GQD effect of F-NiFe PBA to increase the OER catalytic performance. The F content was decreased significantly by increased GQD concentrations as shown in Fig. 4 c. The double-layer capacitance (Cdl) shows the significantly enhanced ECSA after 1 mL GQD-modified F-NiFe PBA on 0.01 M, in contrast to F-NiFe PBA only, which confirms the role of GQD in synergistic with F- ions for providing additional active site within the NiFe PBA (Fig. 4 d). Thus, the results mentioned above suggest that the 1 mL GQD was the optimum concentration of GQD that enhanced the OER performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XAS and XPS analysis of GQD/F-NiFe PBA
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electronic and local coordination structures of GQD/F-NiFe PBA, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) measurements, including the k2-weighted Fourier-transformed EXAFS R-space (FT-EXAFS) spectra of GQD/F-NiFe PBA with extended X-ray absorption fine structure were carried out [53,54]. As shown in Fig. 5a, the X-ray absorption near edge structures (XANES) of the Ni K-edge of GQD/F-NiFe PBA, F-NiFe PBA, and NiFe PBA, along with the Ni foil and NiO references, were different noticeably from those of NiO reference. The energy position of the photon was relative to the XANES spectra, suggesting some changes, which refer to F-NiFe PBA followed by GQD/F-NiFe PBA. Through the Ni K-edge XANES spectra, we can confirm that the F-doping did not affect Ni. In contrast, after integrating GQD into F-NiFe PBA, the oxidation state of Ni rises from Ni2+ ions to higher valence states, which refers to Ni3+ [55]. The k2-weighted FT-EXAFS R-space analysis was performed to better understand the local structure from radial distancing of GQD/F-NiFe PBA. The NiFe PBA inner shell displayed an average bond length of Ni-O of 1.56 Å. Following the introduction of F-doped to NiFe PBA and the integration of GQD into F-NiFe PBA, consistent notable shifts in the Ni-O bond length were observed (Fig. 5 c). Specifically, the bond length was observed to shift to the longer distance by 1.60 Å and rise to 1.64 Å, respectively, which may indicate the bonding alteration in Ni-O after F-doping and GQD integration in NiFe PBA. Interestingly, similar results were also observed in the NiFe PBA outer shell. While the average Ni-Ni bond was 2.46 Å, both the F-doped and GQDs-modified F-NiFe PBA showed longer Ni-Ni bonds, to 2.51 Å and 2.56 Å, respectively. Thus, it refers to the possibility of a new metal bonding to the Ni, the Ni-F bond. Meanwhile, the longer bond length after the GQD integration indicated additional bonding alteration in GQD/F-NiFe PBA, which confirmed the GQD’s existence [55]. This metal-F bonding implies that the shown long range is caused by the synergetic interactions between metal element Ni, F- ions, and GQD to add some bonding alteration further [56]. Moreover, the Fe K-edge XANES reveals no apparent difference (Fig. 5 b), indicating no apparent changes upon F-doping and GQD modification. This result was similar to the inner and outer shells of NiFe PBA observed in Fe K-edge FT-EXAFS R-space, as presented in Fig. 5 d. Recent studies indicate that introducing F-doped elements has a substantial effect on the Ni metal elements compared with Fe. This suggests the formation of a novel bonding mechanism, specifically the M−F bond between Ni and F. Additionally, the integration of GQD has been observed to affect the metal oxidation process, leading to the oxidation of Ni2+ ions to higher valence states, which is a sign of the presence of Ni3+.

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to examine the surface chemical composition as well as the valence state of F-NiFe PBA, GQD/F-NiFe PBA (Fig. 6 ), and NiFe PBA (Figure S7). The high-resolution Ni 2p spectrum comprises two domain peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 and the corresponding satellite peaks. Fig. S7
a shows that Ni3+ (Ni 2p3/2) of NiFe PBA was originally located at 857.0 eV. Moreover, in Fig. 6 a, the Ni3+ (Ni 2p3/2) for the F-NiFe PBA peak that was originally located at 857.2 eV was shifted about ∼ 0.3 eV to the higher binding energy level of 857.5 eV for GQD/F-NiFe PBA, which reflects the strong interaction between GQD and F- ions on the NiFe PBA surfaces [57–59]. This was similar to the Ni K-edge XANES result, that GQD-modified F-NiFe PBA transformed the higher valence state from Ni2+ to Ni3+. Meanwhile, in the Fe 2p spectrum, the Fe3+ (Fe 2p3/2) of F-NiFe PBA, which originally peaked at 709.7 eV is shifted about ∼ 0.1 eV to 709.8 eV after GQD modified F-NiFe PBA, as shown in Fig. 6 b. The Fe3+ (Fe 2p3/2) peak intensity of NiFe PBA (Fig. S7 b) was originally located at peak 709.6 eV, which may confirm there was no significant different impact on the Fe element similar to Fe K-edge XANES and EXAFS R-space [28,60]. Furthermore, the higher oxidation level and strong interaction may be influenced by the high number of edge-sites of GQDs, providing the ideal circumstances for binding metal elements (Ni and Fe), which suggests enhancing the catalytic activity [28,59]. The peaks of 686.3 eV and 687.0 eV in the F 1 s spectrum for GQD/F-NiFe PBA and F-NiFe PBA (Fig. S7 c) demonstrate the formation of the metal-F bond, and the wide scan spectra reveal the existence of all involved elements and the binding energy over the whole spectrum of GQD/F-NiFe PBA (Fig. S7 d). Based on those characterizations, it can be inferred that fluorine-doped contributed to the surface reconstruction of NiFe PBA surfaces. Furthermore, the contribution of GQD was crucial for generating a higher surface metal oxidation in F-NiFe PBA, as proven by the XAS and XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect of GQD/F-NiFe PBA towards the electrocatalytic OER performance
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical OER performance of as-fabricated samples was evaluated in 1.0 M KOH solution. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the polarization curves of GQD/F-NiFe PBA exhibited an overpotential of 318 mV at the current density of 50 mA cm−2, which was lower than those of NiFe PBA (423 mV), F-NiFe PBA (379 mV), GQD/NiFe PBA (339 mV), and commercial RuO2 (372 mV). Moreover, Figure S8 indicates that higher normalized current density is shown by the GQD/F-NiFe PBA rather than the pristine NiFe PBA [61]. The value possessed by GQD/F-NiFe PBA signify that more active sites are involved. To analyze the electrocatalytic capacity of the five catalysts more clearly, the corresponding overpotential comparison at the current density of 100 mA cm−2 was demonstrated in Fig. 7 b. GQD/F-NiFe PBA shows the lowest overpotential (348 mV) than NiFe PBA (504 mV), F-NiFe PBA (400 mV), GQD/NiFe PBA (367 mV), and commercial RuO2 (412 mV). Meanwhile, the EIS was further performed to explore the effect of OER kinetics on the catalytic reactivity (Fig. 7 c), and the GQD/F-NiFe PBA catalyst exhibited an improved charge transfer ability. The corresponding Tafel slope of GQD/F-NiFe PBA is 34.70 mV dec-1, lower than that of NiFe PBA (144.23 mV dec-1), F-NiFe PBA (82.89 mV dec-1), and GQD/NiFe PBA (41.79 mV dec-1), as demonstrated in Fig. 7 d, and even superior to many other PBA-based materials electrocatalysts in 1.0 M KOH (Tabel S1). To explore the properties of the modified surface for GQD/F-NiFe PBA, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the electrocatalysts was observed on double-layer capacitance (Cdl). In GQD/F-NiFe PBA, the catalyst shows a largely enhanced ECSA of (8.01 mF cm−2) after surface modification (Fig. 7 e), which suggested the promotion of more active sites, which resulted in accelerated OER electrocatalysis kinetics. To further investigate the durability performance of catalysts, chronoamperometry measurements were carried out for 30 h under a high current density of 100 mA cm−2 (Fig. 7 f), proving that GQD/F-NiFe PBA revealed superior durability. A comparative analysis was also performed on the OER performance of the GQD/F-NiFe PBA by a pre-and post-chronoamperometry stability test. Additionally, the physical characterization of the material was undertaken using SEM and XRD techniques to assess any structural alterations in the GQD/F-NiFe PBA summarized in Figure S9. The SEM image of GQD/F-NiFe PBA during the extended duration experiment (Figure S9 b) reveals that the original smaller and irregular structure of GQD/F-NiFe PBA transformed, forming bigger and irregular aggregates. In Figure S9 c, the XRD pattern reveals the occurrence of distinctive peaks of NiFe PBA that exhibit a collapsing behavior. This collapse indicates the formation of a mixed state consisting of Ni and Fe oxides and hydroxide [62–64]. The formation of these states can be attributed to the significant reactivity of the catalyst, leading to the catalyst’s transition to a higher oxidation state for both nickel (Ni) and iron (Fe). Following a thoughtful discussion, the results of the XPS study point to the Ni3+/Fe3+ acting as the active site of the GQD/F-NiFe PBA. And it was shown when GQD was introduced to F-NiFe PBA. A high surface oxidation state had been triggered, which led to the reduction of Ni2+ ions to Ni3+ ions and the oxidation of Fe2+ ions to Fe3+ ions, activating the electrocatalysis active sites for the early established OER process [65–67]. All the results indicated that GQD/F-NiFe PBA, as an outstanding electrocatalyst, can accelerate reaction kinetics and charge transfer rate ascribed to the surface modification.

In conclusion, GQD/F-NiFe PBA was successfully synthesized through the tandem surface reconstruction of F doping and GQD modification on NiFe PBA surfaces without changing the PBA framework. GQD/F-NiFe PBA enhances Ni3+/Fe3+ as active sites, ultimately enhancing early-triggered OER performance. The F doping leads to the reconstruction of the NiFe PBA surface by the strong electronegativity of the F- ions, which leads to the stronger Ni-F bond. This results in a slight surface expansion without changing the core NiFe PBA structure, as demonstrated by XRD patterns. On the other hand, the GQD was found to easily alter the F-NiFe PBA by activating the metal states to enhance electrical conductivity. This research study reveals that the interaction between F- ions and GQD plays a crucial role in modulating the electronic and local structure of the main transition metal in PBA, as presented in XAS and XPS results. This synergistic interaction leads to an enhancement in the electrocatalytic efficiency of PBA towards OER performance. After understanding and optimization of F doping and GQD modification on the surface of NiFe PBA, the electrochemical performance of GQD/F-NiFe PBA, which exhibits an OER overpotential of 318 mV at 50 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH with a small Tafel slope of 34.70 mV dec-1 and an excellent durability test performance at a high current density of 100 mA cm−2 for 30 h. The proposed strategy for modifying catalyst surfaces, which involves hosting GQD/F-NiFe PBA on catalytically active support, represents a novel approach to designing advanced catalysts.
